seo.title: Jamaica Payroll Tax Calendar 2026-2027: Every Monthly Deadline Employers Need
seo.description: Complete Jamaica payroll tax calendar for April 2026 to March 2027. Monthly PAYE, NIS, NHT, and Education Tax deadlines, annual return dates, public holidays, and penalty rates for late filing.

title: Jamaica Payroll Tax Calendar 2026-2027: Every Monthly Deadline Employers Need
subtitle: A month-by-month guide to every PAYE, NIS, NHT, and Education Tax deadline for the April 2026 – March 2027 tax year — so you never pay a late penalty again.

ctaInline.headline: Never Miss a Payroll Deadline
ctaInline.text: Payroll Jamaica sends automatic reminders before every filing date and calculates your PAYE, NIS, NHT, and Education Tax — try it free

ctaBottom.headline: Automate Your Entire Tax Calendar

html:
Missing a payroll filing deadline in Jamaica does not just cost you a penalty — it can trigger TAJ audits, NIS benefit disputes, and NHT compliance reviews. Yet many employers still rely on memory or scribbled notes to track four separate monthly remittances, plus annual returns.

This calendar gives you every single payroll tax deadline for the April 2026 to March 2027 Jamaican tax year. Bookmark it, print it, or better yet — let payroll software handle the reminders automatically.

Understanding Jamaica's Payroll Tax Year

Jamaica's tax year runs from 1 April to 31 March. The 2026-2027 tax year begins on Wednesday 1 April 2026 and ends on Wednesday 31 March 2027. All annual returns reference this period, not the calendar year.

For employers, four statutory deductions must be remitted monthly:

  • PAYE (Income Tax) — withheld from employees earning above the JMD 1,500,096 annual threshold
  • Education Tax — 2.25% from employees, 3.5% from employers
  • NIS (National Insurance Scheme) — 3% from employees, 3% from employers on insurable earnings
  • NHT (National Housing Trust) — 2% from employees, 3% from employers

All four are due by the 14th of the month following the pay period. When the 14th falls on a weekend or public holiday, payment is due on the last business day before the 14th.

Monthly PAYE and Education Tax Remittance

PAYE and Education Tax are filed together with Tax Administration Jamaica (TAJ) using the monthly employer's return.

Key details:

  • Due date: 14th of the following month
  • Filed with: Tax Administration Jamaica (TAJ) via the eTax portal at etax.gov.jm or in person
  • Form: Monthly employer's return (SO1)
  • Late payment penalty: Interest at the prescribed rate on the outstanding amount, plus a fixed surcharge for each month the return is late
  • Failure to file penalty: Up to JMD 5,000 per employee per month — this adds up quickly for larger employers

Use the free Jamaica payroll calculator to verify your PAYE and Education Tax calculations before filing.

Monthly NIS Remittance

NIS contributions fund employee benefits including sickness, maternity, and retirement pensions.

  • Due date: 14th of the following month
  • Filed with: The National Insurance Fund, Ministry of Labour and Social Security
  • Combined rate: 6% total (3% employer + 3% employee) on insurable earnings up to the ceiling
  • Late payment consequence: Employees may lose benefit eligibility if contributions are not recorded on time — this creates personal liability for the employer

Monthly NHT Remittance

NHT contributions are mandatory for all employed persons in Jamaica.

  • Due date: 14th of the following month
  • Filed with: National Housing Trust (nht.gov.jm)
  • Combined rate: 5% total (3% employer + 2% employee) on gross emoluments
  • Late payment penalty: Surcharges apply, and persistent non-compliance can trigger enforcement action from NHT

Annual Returns and Year-End Deadlines

In addition to monthly filings, employers must submit annual returns at the end of the tax year.

ReturnFiled WithDeadlinePurpose
SO1 Annual ReturnTAJ31 March each yearSummary of all PAYE and Education Tax deducted during the tax year
P24 CertificateIssued to employees30 April (after tax year ends)Employee tax deduction certificate showing total earnings and deductions
NHT Annual ReturnNHT31 March each yearSummary of all NHT contributions for the tax year
NIS Annual ReturnNIS Office31 March each yearConfirmation of all NIS contributions per employee

The March 31 deadline is the single most important date on the Jamaican payroll calendar. Missing it can result in penalties, rejected employee benefit claims, and compliance flags on your TAJ account.

Month-by-Month Calendar: April 2026 – March 2027

Below is every payroll deadline for the 2026-2027 tax year. For each month, we list what is due and the exact date.

DateWhat's DueNotes

Jamaica Public Holidays That May Affect Filing Dates (2026-2027)

When a filing deadline falls on or near a public holiday, you must file before the holiday. Here are the public holidays for the 2026-2027 tax year:

DateHolidayImpact on Filing
23 May 2026 (Sat)Labour DayNo direct impact — 14th already passed
1 Aug 2026 (Sat)Emancipation DayNo direct impact on the 14th
6 Aug 2026 (Thu)Independence DayFile early if your workflow depends on office visits
19 Oct 2026 (Mon)National Heroes DayNo impact — deadline is the 14th
25 Dec 2026 (Fri)Christmas DayNo impact on filing — but December payroll may include bonus calculations
26 Dec 2026 (Sat)Boxing DayNo direct impact
1 Jan 2027 (Fri)New Year's DayNo impact on the 14th
26 Mar 2027 (Fri)Good Friday (estimated)Falls before March 31 — file annual returns before the Easter break
29 Mar 2027 (Mon)Easter Monday (estimated)Critical: Only 2 business days between Easter Monday and March 31 — do not wait

Penalties for Missing Deadlines

The financial consequences of late filing are severe and compound quickly:

  • Late PAYE/Education Tax payment: Interest charged at the prescribed rate (historically around 40% per annum) on outstanding amounts, plus a fixed surcharge per month of non-compliance
  • Late NIS contribution: Employees lose access to NIS benefits (sickness, maternity, injury) for periods with missing contributions — this creates employer liability for claims employees cannot make
  • Late NHT contribution: Surcharges and potential legal action from NHT, plus employees cannot claim NHT refunds or housing benefits
  • Missing the SO1 Annual Return: TAJ penalty plus the employer is flagged for potential audit review
  • Missing P24 issuance: Employees cannot file their own personal income tax returns, which may lead to complaints to TAJ that trigger employer scrutiny

7 Tips for Never Missing a Payroll Deadline

1. Set calendar alerts for the 7th of every month

Give yourself a full week before the 14th to prepare returns and arrange payments. Waiting until the 13th leaves no margin for errors or bank processing delays.

2. Use electronic filing whenever possible

TAJ's eTax portal, NHT's online system, and NIS electronic filing are all faster and provide instant confirmation. Paper returns depend on postal delivery and manual processing.

3. Separate your payroll tax funds

Maintain a dedicated bank account for statutory deductions. Transfer PAYE, NIS, NHT, and Education Tax amounts into this account on payday — not on the filing date. This prevents cash flow surprises.

4. Reconcile monthly, not annually

Do not wait until March to discover a discrepancy. Compare your payroll records against TAJ, NIS, and NHT receipts every month. A small error in April becomes a large penalty by March.

5. Start your annual return in January

The March 31 deadline catches many employers off guard. Begin compiling your SO1 annual return, NHT return, and P24 certificates no later than January. This gives you two full months to resolve any discrepancies.

6. Account for public holidays near the 14th

When the 14th falls on a weekend or public holiday, the effective deadline moves earlier. Check this calendar at the start of each month and adjust your schedule accordingly.

7. Automate with payroll software

The most reliable way to never miss a deadline is to remove manual tracking entirely. Payroll Jamaica automatically calculates every deduction, generates return-ready reports, and sends deadline reminders before the 14th of every month. It tracks the entire tax calendar so you do not have to.

Visit our blog for more guides on Jamaica payroll compliance, or try the free payroll calculator to verify your deductions right now.
